sentences.text: Good afternoon.
Good afternoon.
My name is Abraham Bomba, B-O-M-B-A.
Yes.
No.
My birthday is June the 9th, 1913.
83 years old.
I was born in Beuthen Oberschlesien.
B-E-U-T-H-E-N.
That's the city.
Germany.
That is located on the border of Poland, next to Katowice.
No.
I grew up in Poland, in Czenstochowa.
C-Z-E-N-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A.
That's in Polish spelling.
Like-- just like any other city in Poland.
There were all kind people, all kind organization.
Mostly which I attended was Jewish places, Jewish organizations.
We went to school over there.
We had special public school for Jewish children and for non-Jewish, which I attended the Jewish schools.
Just the public school from that city.
Six.
Yes, yes.
Before noon-- that mean from 8:00 to about 12:00 we went to cheder, which is a Jewish educational school.
We learned of the Bible, or other study of the Jewish history and of the Jewish Talmud.
In afternoon, I went to a public school, which started at 1 o'clock-- 1 o'clock.
And it finished sometimes seven, sometimes 8 o'clock at night.
The entire population, it was around 135,000.
And Jewish people was around 35,000, 38,000 at that time.
They were scattered, but not in a big group.
Mostly the Jewish area was the lower part of the city, in another word also the poorest part of the city in Czenstochowa.
Yes.
Yes.
Beginning was under whatever it is called.
[INAUDIBLE].
Now, that mean under water.
And then I lived on [INAUDIBLE], which is mean under old marketplace.
Oh, it was apartment.
Well, describe-- there's nothing much to describe.
It was a very small apartment, very small apartment.
As a matter of fact, there was only one room, the whole apartment.
How many people lived in that apartment?
Five.
We were from the one, not the rich one, not the fortunate one, but the working class, where we couldn't afford any better.
Bernard.
In Jewish, [?
Bertle. ?]
Bomba.
He was a helper manufacturing shoes.
No.
He worked for somebody.
He hardly, he hardly worked, because it was hard to get a job, especially in Poland.
He was a very intelligent man, I would say.
Fluently in German, Polish, Jewish, Russian.
And he was a very nice looking man.
I would say so.
And he-- he was.
Also, he spent seven and a half years in the Russia army at that time.
So he was somebody.
No, I wouldn't say special.
The only thing is that he taught me how to behave, how to be a human being, how to get education.
Even-- he said, I work like that-- even when you don't keep it, like religion or other thing, but you should know.
When you come together with people, you should know how to open up your mouth.
My mother's name was Touba.
Touba and Feigl.
But we called her Touba.
Well, everybody called her Touba.
Her maiden name was Kozuch.
No, she never worked.
It's very hard for me to describe her, because she was a lovely, nice looking woman.
And she was the real Jewish mother.
Like to say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
What I mean, she did everything for the children.
You know, I remember-- I think she was at her years, which when she passed away, she was almost 50 years old.
And when we-- in the house, we were little, I mean, 12, 13 years old, she wouldn't let nobody to do anything she did, like bringing in wood, coal, other thing.
She would do it.
And she was something special in cooking.
Really special in cooking.
She could make from potatoes in water, she could make a meal like for a king.
I don't know how she did it.
Everything she make.
Everything she maked, which wasn't too much to make.
It was many, many nights we went to sleep without food.
Yes.
I had a brother and sister in Paris.
I never met them.
It happened that my father's first wife passed away.
She was sick.
She had cancer.
And after quite a time-- I don't know exactly when-- he met my mother.
And they married, and I was the one who was born from them.
Yes.
His name was Henry, and her name is Rachel.
Last name was Bomba.
Her name was Chatkine after her marriage.
Yes, I had another brother, Jacob, and a younger brother, Judah.
They was with my mother.
My brother was a little older than I was.
He was a very nice gentleman.
He worked very hard to help out in the house, to bring in-- because when my father passed away, we were almost children.
About 16 years.
And we had a little boy also.
He was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six or seven years old.
And hardly was food in the house.
So when he started to grown up, and he went to work, so he helped out a lot in the house with everything.
When he started working, we had almost everything in the house.
I don't mean everything, you know, like comfortable.
But at least we had food in the house.
Yes.
No, none of them.
None of them.
As a matter of fact, I'm named after my grandfather.
Avrum Leib.
That's my name.
When I was growing up, I was, according to my father, I wasn't too religious, but I know the Bible.
I know the Bible by heart and still up to now.
The thing is I grew up to be a Jew, a good Jew, a fair Jew, but to be a Jew.
I never, never in my life thought that being in a party, like anti-religious party or a Communist Party or the Socialist Party, would give us Jews any survival or any help.
One thing we could do is we've got to have our own state.
And that is the state of Israel, which since I was, I would say, 10 or 12 years, I belong to-- to the Youth Organization of the Zionist.
And then I belonged to the Zionist organization.
The name of the organization was Poale Zion, which today they don't exist.
But they got their name in Israel, Mapai, which the Ben-Gurion organization.
Yeah, I was active in that organization.
All.
All the holidays, not only Shabbos, every holiday.
A holiday in our house was a real holiday.
Well, Shabbos was a special day.
Nobody went to work.
We would dress in the best clothes, what we had.
We went to shul.
From the shul, we came home.
We ate whatever we got.
And then, in the afternoon sometime, we were-- we went to cheder or we went to shul to say a special prayer.
Saturday afternoon, there's a special prayer we say-- [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means how to behave.
She tried to make a meal.
It should be different from the whole week, if it was money-wise.
But she always did something, depends on what she could.
The most important thing was a cholent.
What come, potatoes, meat, a bone, a little fattening.
And we were waiting for that for the Saturday meal.
That was Friday afternoon.
Saturday, after 1:00, 2 o'clock, after we came from the shul.
And it was prepared.
Yes.
You didn't buy any challah.
At that time, we didn't know about the-- had to make-- make challah or to go to-- everyone did it.
Oh, Pesach was a special holiday for Jewish people, not only for myself, but for all the Jewish people.
It was something, which is like today to see that would be like a dream.
Because first of all, when my father was alive-- that was the really thing-- he was dressed like a king.
White.
White-- like with a white garment all of it.
And special place to sit, a special-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how you call-- utilities to prepare the food, because there was-- it has to be kosher, the Pesach.
It cannot be the same what you use the whole year around.
Oh, this was baked by bakers.
We bought it.
Nobody did for themself, because that's a tough job to do and also not allowed to do.
It has to be by a rabbi, special, the oven and the place where they baked it.
No private, nobody did, not any that I know of.
Nobody did.
We had bakeries that did it.
Yes.
It was special cleaned, special painted for it.
Drink. ?] So it should look different than the whole year around.
Not special.
I also made food and prepared this.
We go to bring water in, because we didn't have the water in the house, like they have it now.
And it was a job also to bring it over, a job to prepare the oven.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, we had that.
It was like from the people that lived in the same building, which wasn't too big.
It was about six, seven tenants in the same building.
We had a small sukkah.
And we had it over there.
So we ate in that sukkah.
Oh, favorite holiday, I would say, Rosh Hashanah--
--was my favorite, because it was the new year.
The new year starts.
And the joy between people.
It was wishing everybody a good year.
That was-- that was a favorite, yeah.
Yes, I had a bar mitzvah.
Not-- I had not a huge bar mitzvah, because we were poor people.
We didn't celebrate it.
My father took me in to the shul.
And I make-- there wasn't even on a Saturday.
And it wasn't-- just he took me in.
He showed me how to put on tefillin, if you know what that means.
That is, you know, they put around.
It make from leather, and also on the hat, and make the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's all it was.
I-- I don't even remember [? oh ?] we had whiskey or something to it, because we had no money for it.
That was the bar mitzvah, not the bar mitzvah that is today, which cost-- [INAUDIBLE] I would say-- $10,000, $20,000, $50,000, [? using ?] with that.
Oh, we had synagogue, quite a few.
But mostly they prayed is in shtiblach.
What shtiblach means, a little prayer house.
Not exactly a synagogue, a little prayer house, private.
People were there, two or three a room.
They converted one room for the Torah and for the private prayers in the evening.
In mine little house, where I lived, between seven tenants, there was also a little shtiebel, which we made the prayers Friday night, Saturday in that place.
But we had synagogues, beautiful synagogues.
One of the most beautiful was the-- we called it the German synagogue.
That's-- that's the name was all the time.
Why?
I don't know.
Where for holiday, at that time, people used to come with horses and wagon-- [INAUDIBLE] beautiful wagon.
[INAUDIBLE] they called it-- to the synagogue.
High cylinder hats.
Very, very rich, very famous people.
As a matter of fact, today, they make, from that synagogue, a museum, and also a opera house from that right now.
Yes, in-- in Czenstochowa, same place.
Then we had a synagogue.
They called it the old synagogue.
The old synagogue was for people, common people, not the very rich, not the very poor.
Over there, we had special canters.
And it was make something, something like a dream.
I got a friend of mine, which I will talk about him later, who was with me in Treblinka, his father painted it.
He took eight years.
Not just painting.
No, the pictures created on the ceiling and on the walls, they were masterpieces.
It's unbelievable.
No, it was destroyed in the first days.
Yes, yes.
Yes.
We had institution to help poor people.
We had doctors.
Somebody was sick, they went to, didn't have to pay anything, with medicine.
We had institution like very, very poor people, they couldn't afford to make a meal out.
So we had an institution that supported them, that brought in food for them.
We had also an institution to marry off young girls, which they had no diary-- dowry.

And they took-- I don't know from where-- and they clothed them, and they married them off, which call, in our language, Hachnasat Cala, to bring in the [? caller ?] that she shouldn't be ashamed, as your married.
Then we had also an institution for a few people.
They had no place where to sleep.
They call it Hachnasat Orchim That is, I guess, people that came from somewhere, people, beggars.
They have no place where to go.
They slept over there, and they had their meals over there.
It was supported by the Jewish people, by the richer Jewish people, who could afford.
And there was also sent-- set a tax from the kehilla.
Kehilla means the committee of the Jewish people.
They taxed the people-- they know who so much and so much-- to help out the poor people.
Yes, I had-- I had a Jewish friend.
My relation-- my special relation was very good, excellent was.
Before the war, I had a lot of friends.
We went out together.
And even I will tell a story about the Holocaust.
I will tell you the story about non-Jewish people, which I have.
Yes.
Yes.
The-- the Polish people, which they didn't live in our section, in the Jewish section mostly.
There were-- no, we had fights with them, throwing stones, and calling names each other.
Oh, we went through-- we went through that, because they were agitated by priest, by teachers, anti-Semitic.
And I would say it wasn't even the fault of the kids.
They heard of it.
And they didn't understand anything else.
But the Jewish people killed Jesus, which nobody of us know what that is.
And from that, it started, the anti-Semitism about the young people.
I don't know.
It's hard for me to describe myself.
I was more than the shy side.
Yeah, I was more shy.
I involved in-- in sport.
Mostly soccer games.
That most-- most of it.
I could never an ambition, because I know mine ambition will not go through, because I couldn't afford it.
Yes, I had ambition.
I had ambition to be a doctor.
No.
Of course, we knew.
The papers.
We had papers with everything.
And then in the '30s, then we had radio around also.
Even in 1932, '33, we had a radio in our own house.
Yes.
My-- my-- my parent-- my father was not alive.
My mother-- she did not too much about it, because for a woman, politics-- for a Jewish woman, especially, it was the high class to discuss politic.
Oh, I tell you.

I was thinking that they were talking a lot, but actually they would not do what they did, because we couldn't believe it that they're gonna to do to kill people, to cremate people, or-- or poison them or kill them.
Which is in this book, what he wrote, being in jail in 1923-- he was jailed.
He wrote that book in jail.
So actually, we didn't-- we didn't think too much about it.
It began to change in the late '30s.
I tell you why.
Because the Polish government and the Polish people, they leaned on the German and the Nazis.
And their word was like that, just wait until Hitler will come.
Then we will teach you a lessons, to the Jewish people.
So that changed altogether.
As you wish.
